data.body:
Research (web) backing the a278 T10 endpoint_access_contract check: how actively-maintained
security / LLM-security-review projects distinguish "needs auth" from "legitimately no auth"
and avoid false positives. Feeds the calibrated criterion that flagged the Gerrit gap (d251)
6/6 while staying 0/4 FP on local no-network work.

## Key distinguishing principles (with sources)
1. TRUST-BOUNDARY CROSSING is the gate for whether auth is even in scope. A component needs
   authn only if reachable by an actor at a LOWER trust level (internet, another tenant,
   untrusted network, unauthenticated user). STRIDE: the Spoofing threat "applies to external
   entities and processes and is mitigated by authentication." An in-process library call / local
   CLI does not cross a trust boundary => no Spoofing threat => no auth requirement. This is the
   crisp justification for PASSing local/no-network components. (Drata/Security Compass STRIDE.)
2. APPLICABILITY IS ARCHITECTURE-DEPENDENT — requirements don't apply uniformly. OWASP ASVS:
   "Certain verification requirements may not be applicable" (e.g. a stateless service API w/o a
   client makes much of V3 Session Mgmt N/A); enforcement is verified only at "trusted enforcement
   points ... access control gateways, servers, and serverless functions" (ASVS 1.4.1); level
   selection is risk-based (public+PII => L2/L3; internal+low-impact => L1). "Is this reachable by
   untrusted actors?" is the standard's own gate.
3. REACHABILITY / EXPLOITABILITY — not category membership — makes a finding real. Semgrep:
   reachability analysis cuts FPs on high/critical findings "by up to 98%." Anthropic's reviewer:
   "Severity is assigned per finding based on exploitability in your codebase, not the category
   itself"; a code finding "does not automatically tell you whether the vulnerable endpoint is
   exposed externally, whether auth or gateway controls make exploitation impractical, or whether
   the vulnerable code path is reachable." An auth finding on an unreachable/local component is,
   by this standard, a false positive. (Semgrep whitepaper; anthropics/claude-code-security-review.)
4. "UNDECLARED AUTH POSTURE IS ITSELF THE DEFECT" is a codified pattern = secure/deny-by-default
   made auditable. OWASP Proactive Controls C5 / Authorization Cheat Sheet: "Access should be
   denied by default and only granted through an allowed list" — an ABSENT decision defaults closed,
   so an unspecified auth posture on a reachable service is a failure, not a neutral gap. ASVS 1.1.4:
   "Verify documentation and JUSTIFICATION of all the application's trust boundaries, components,
   and significant data flows" — i.e. the boundary decision must be documented+justified. That is
   exactly the "auth mechanism OR explicit justified no-auth" rule. (Reinforced by PEP20 "explicit
   is better than implicit"; OWASP "fail secure / fail closed".)

## Prompt-text patterns adapted (the FP-avoidance levers)
- SCOPE GATE: raise an authn/authz finding ONLY if the component crosses a trust boundary
  (reachable by a lower-trust actor); in-process/same-trust callers (imported library, local CLI,
  in-process helper) => respond NOT_APPLICABLE, no finding.
- REACHABILITY REQUIREMENT: before flagging missing auth, name the concrete entry point an
  untrusted actor could reach (non-loopback listener, HTTP route, queue consumer, public cloud
  ingress); if none, treat as FP. Judge exploitability in THIS codebase, not by category.
- EXPLICIT-DECISION / DENY-BY-DEFAULT: every network-reachable service must make an explicit auth
  decision — a named mechanism OR a justified no-auth (loopback-only, behind a gateway,
  single-tenant private net). Absent/ambiguous auth on a reachable service is a BLOCK-level defect.
- POSITIVE-PASS CARVE-OUT (stops over-broadening onto local tools): pure libraries, in-process
  modules, single-user local CLIs, build/dev tooling, loopback-only surfaces are legitimately
  auth-free — PASS, do not recommend auth.
- SELF-ADJUDICATION: after drafting each auth finding, try to DISPROVE it (gateway / mTLS / network
  policy / loopback already enforcing the boundary; is it actually reachable?) and drop if unsustained.

## Deterministic signals (encodable — belong to the CODE-review gate on *.tf/config diffs, not plan prose)
1. Non-loopback / public bind (0.0.0.0, ::, public IP vs 127.0.0.1/localhost/unix socket).
2. Public ingress / SG open to 0.0.0.0/0 (tfsec aws-vpc-no-public-ingress-sgr; Checkov CKV_AWS_* SG).
3. Fronted by a public LB / public ingress resource / public IP / API Gateway route (tfsec no-public-ingress family).
4. Declares HTTP/RPC routes or a remote-accepting queue consumer (a real request-handling surface).
5. Handles identity / secrets / privileged / state-changing / cross-tenant operations (STRIDE EoP/Spoofing).
   Negative (auto-pass) signals: loopback-only bind / unix socket; no listener (pure lib/CLI);
   importable module with no served port; SG sourced from a private CIDR / named SG (not /0).

## Pitfalls in over-broadening (why NOT to hard-code naive rules)
- "Internal" != "no auth needed" (zero-trust: a private network can still hold untrusted/other-tenant
  actors) — treat as LOWER severity, not exempt.
- Loopback/private bind can be undone by deployment (127.0.0.1 in code but 0.0.0.0 via
  compose/env) — flag when the bind is externally configurable without a safe default.
- Existing controls may live OUTSIDE the diff (gateway, service-mesh mTLS, network policy) — look
  for / ask about a boundary control before BLOCKing.
- Category-based flagging (fires on "auth"/"network" keywords) is the classic FP generator — gate on
  reachability, not vocabulary.
- The "explicit decision" rule itself over-fires if applied to every module — gate it behind the
  reachability/trust-boundary signals.

## Sources
OWASP ASVS (project + V1 Architecture 1.1.4/1.4.1); OWASP Proactive Controls C5 (Secure by Default);
OWASP Authorization Cheat Sheet (deny by default); OWASP Developer Guide Security Principles;
OWASP Top 10 2025 A01 / 2021 A07; STRIDE (Drata, Security Compass); Semgrep reachability whitepaper +
zero-FP AI memory blog; anthropics/claude-code-security-review + Anthropic Claude Code security
announcement; Checkmarx Claude Code security write-up; Crash Override "Prompting LLM security reviews";
tfsec no-public-ingress(-sgr) (AWS/GCP); Checkov/tfsec SG checks; arXiv LLM4FPM 2411.03079, ZeroFalse 2510.02534.
